output_text:
Summary: Flagged two load-bearing internal contradictions, then produced a revised draft that resolves them on the assumption the supervised training description is correct.

## Critical issues to fix before any rewrite

1. **Supervision contradiction (must resolve).** §3 says you fine-tune the rewriter "on AmbigNQ disambiguated questions" with "gold disambiguated (question, answer) pairs." §4 then claims the gains come from a "supervision-free approach" where the model "never sees gold AmbigNQ disambiguations during training." These cannot both be true. A reviewer will catch this immediately. Decide which is the actual setup and rewrite §4's causal claim accordingly.

2. **End-to-end contradiction (must resolve).** The abstract sells "the first end-to-end generative model... a single seq2seq backbone that performs all three steps in one forward pass." §2 then describes four separately-trained components (DPR + classifier + T5-large rewriter + FiD reader), "trained sequentially, each with its own objective, and combined at inference time." That is a pipeline, not an end-to-end single-backbone system. Either (a) drop the "end-to-end / single backbone" framing, or (b) describe joint training if that is what you actually do.

3. **Unsupported causal claim.** "We attribute these gains to..." needs an ablation; otherwise replace with "We hypothesize" and move to discussion.

4. **"First" overclaim.** AmbigQA / SpanSeqGen (Min et al., 2020) and REFUEL (Gao et al., 2021) already address generative ambiguity-aware QA. "First end-to-end generative model" is probably indefensible. Soften or qualify precisely (e.g., "first to jointly model X and Y in a single decoder").

5. **Missing baseline detail.** The +5.3 F1_ans is reported only against SpanSeqGen, on dev only. Add test-set numbers, additional baselines (REFUEL, DPR+FiD reader), and either confidence intervals or seed variance.

6. **Minor.** "DPR-style" is vague — name the encoder and whether the index is the December 2018 Wikipedia dump used by AmbigNQ, for comparability. "21M passages" should match prior work's split for the comparison to be apples-to-apples.

## Revised draft (assumes the supervised training in §3 is correct; pipeline, not end-to-end)

---

Open-domain QA is typically evaluated as a single-answer prediction task, even though a large fraction of natural-language questions admit multiple valid interpretations: Min et al. (2020) report that roughly half of NQ-Open questions sampled in AmbigNQ are ambiguous under their annotation protocol. Collapsing these to one answer both under-reports system competence and hides interpretive choices from the user.

We present DISAMBIG-FiD, a retrieval-augmented system for ambiguity-aware open-domain QA that detects question ambiguity, enumerates plausible interpretations as disambiguated questions, and produces an answer for each. The system is a four-stage pipeline: (i) a DPR retriever over the December 2018 Wikipedia dump (21M passages, matching AmbigNQ) returns the top-100 passages; (ii) a classifier head over the retrieved set predicts whether the question is ambiguous; (iii) a T5-large rewriter emits one disambiguated question per interpretation; and (iv) a fusion-in-decoder reader answers each rewrite independently. Stages are trained separately with their own objectives and composed at inference.

All components are fine-tuned on the AmbigNQ training split (Min et al., 2020). The retriever uses the standard DPR contrastive objective; the classifier is trained with binary cross-entropy on AmbigNQ ambiguity labels; the rewriter is supervised on AmbigNQ gold disambiguated questions; the reader is trained on the resulting (rewrite, answer) pairs.

On the AmbigNQ dev split, DISAMBIG-FiD reaches 48.1 F1_ans (all) and 41.7 F1_BLEU on disambiguated questions, a 5.3 F1_ans improvement over SpanSeqGen (Min et al., 2020). Test-set numbers and a per-stage ablation isolating the contribution of explicit ambiguity classification appear in §5.

DISAMBIG-FiD is a step toward QA systems that surface, rather than collapse, the multiple legitimate interpretations of a user's query. Code and checkpoints will be released on acceptance.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: I assumed the supervised training description in §3 is the ground truth and that the "supervision-free" sentence in §4 was a drafting error; if the opposite is true, the revision needs to be redone. I also assumed your index matches the AmbigNQ Wikipedia dump — verify the dump date. The AmbigNQ ambiguity prevalence figure ("roughly half") is from memory of Min et al. (2020); confirm against the paper before submission. The claim that REFUEL (Gao et al., 2021) exists and is relevant is from memory — verify the citation.
